The paper search didn't return directly relevant amyloid kinetics results, but the Knowles/Cohen master equation and Brichos-selective-secondary-nucleation mechanism are well-established (Cohen et al. PNAS 2015; Arosio et al.). The structural principle is clear and matches the breakthrough idea: inhibitors like Brichos act selectively on the fibril-surface secondary nucleation channel, changing sigmoid steepness/half-time (shape) without altering the plateau. I'll implement it directly.

The key structural change:
- Keep seed nucleation (M0) and primary nucleation (m0) uninhibited
- Autocatalytic channels: linear elongation `k2²·m0·c` + secondary nucleation `k5²·m0·c²`
- Both autocatalytic channels share inhibitor suppression `1/(1+k3²·cd)`
- Bounded `(plateau - c)` capacity factor for mass conservation

This is 6 rate/plateau constants (k0..k5) = well under 13. c is bounded so c² ≤ c → no overflow; denominator ≥ 1 → no singularities.

- 6 constants total (well under 13), well-conditioned; linear/quadratic autocatalytic terms separated by their c-power to avoid collinearity, with k5 initialized small (0.3) to break the tie cleanly.

This addresses the identified limitation: the previous uniform-inhibition template rescaled trajectories in time uniformly, missing kinetic shape. The selective secondary-nucleation term should produce sharper, better-timed onset, reducing the shape_loss while keeping NMSE stable.
